* In *[The Map of Time](/wiki/The_Map_of_Time "The Map of Time")* (2008) by Spanish author [Félix J. Palma](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_J._Palma "Félix J. Palma"); Wells is one of several historical characters.[[160]](#cite_note-160)
* Wells is one of the two Georges in [Paul Levinson](/wiki/Paul_Levinson "Paul Levinson")'s 2013 time-travel novelette, "Ian, George, and George", published in *[Analog](/wiki/Analog_Science_Fiction_and_Fact "Analog Science Fiction and Fact")* magazine.[[161]](#cite_note-161)
* [David Lodge](/wiki/David_Lodge_(author) "David Lodge (author)")'s novel *A Man of Parts* (2011) is a literary retelling of the life of Wells.[[162]](#cite_note-162)

### Dramatic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=H._G._Wells&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Dramatic")]

* [Rod Taylor](/wiki/Rod_Taylor "Rod Taylor") portrays Wells[[163]](#cite_note-163)[[164]](#cite_note-palumbo-164) in the 1960 [science fiction film](/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film") *[The Time Machine](/wiki/The_Time_Machine_(1960_film) "The Time Machine (1960 film)")* (based on the novel of the same name), in which Wells uses his time machine to try to find his Utopian society.[[164]](#cite_note-palumbo-164)
* [Malcolm McDowell](/wiki/Malcolm_McDowell "Malcolm McDowell") portrays Wells in the 1979 science fiction film *[Time After Time](/wiki/Time_After_Time_(1979_film) "Time After Time (1979 film)")*, in which Wells uses a time machine to pursue [Jack the Ripper](/wiki/Jack_the_Ripper "Jack the Ripper") to the present day.[[164]](#cite_note-palumbo-164) In the film, Wells meets "Amy" in the future who then returns to 1893 to become his second wife Amy Catherine Robbins.
* Wells is portrayed in the 1985 story *[Timelash](/wiki/Timelash "Timelash")* from the [22nd season](/wiki/Doctor_Who_(season_22) "Doctor Who (season 22)") of the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") science-fiction television series *[Doctor Who](/wiki/Doctor_Who "Doctor Who")*. In this story, Herbert, an enthusiastic temporary companion to the Doctor, is revealed to be a young H. G. Wells. The plot is loosely based upon the themes and characters of *[The Time Machine](/wiki/The_Time_Machine "The Time Machine")* with references to *[The War of the Worlds](/wiki/The_War_of_the_Worlds "The War of the Worlds")*, *[The Invisible Man](/wiki/The_Invisible_Man "The Invisible Man")* and *[The Island of Doctor Moreau](/wiki/The_Island_of_Doctor_Moreau "The Island of Doctor Moreau")*. The story jokingly suggests that Wells's inspiration for his later novels came from his adventure with the [Sixth Doctor](/wiki/Sixth_Doctor "Sixth Doctor").[[165]](#cite_note-165)
* In the British TV miniseries *[The Infinite Worlds of H. G. Wells](/wiki/The_Infinite_Worlds_of_H._G._Wells "The Infinite Worlds of H. G. Wells")* (2001), several of Wells's short stories are dramatised but are adapted using Wells himself (Tom Ward) as the main protagonist in each story.[[166]](#cite_note-166)
* In the Disney Channel Original Series *[Phil of the Future](/wiki/Phil_of_the_Future "Phil of the Future")*, which centres on time-travel, the present-day high school that the main characters attend is named "H. G. Wells".[[167]](#cite_note-167)
* In the 2006 television docudrama *[H. G. Wells: War with the World](/wiki/H._G._Wells:_War_with_the_World "H. G. Wells: War with the World")*, Wells is played by [Michael Sheen](/wiki/Michael_Sheen "Michael Sheen").[[168]](#cite_note-168)
* Television episode "World's End" of Cold Case (2007) is about how the discovery of human remains in the bottom of a well leads to the reinvestigation of the case of a housewife who went missing during Orson Welles' radio broadcast of "War of the Worlds".[[169]](#cite_note-169)
* On the science fiction television series *[Warehouse 13](/wiki/Warehouse_13 "Warehouse 13")* (2009–2014), there is a female version Helena G. Wells. When she appeared, she explained that her brother was her front for her writing because a female science fiction author would not be accepted.[[170]](#cite_note-170)
* Comedian [Paul F. Tompkins](/wiki/Paul_F._Tompkins "Paul F. Tompkins") portrays a fictional Wells as the host of *[The Dead Authors Podcast](/wiki/The_Dead_Authors_Podcast "The Dead Authors Podcast")*, wherein Wells uses his time machine to bring dead authors (played by other comedians) to the present and interview them.[[171]](#cite_note-171)[[172]](#cite_note-172)
* In the four-part series *[The Nightmare Worlds of H. G. Wells](/wiki/The_Nightmare_Worlds_of_H._G._Wells "The Nightmare Worlds of H. G. Wells")* (2016), Wells is played by [Ray Winstone](/wiki/Ray_Winstone "Ray Winstone").[[173]](#cite_note-173)
* In the 2017 television series version of *[Time After Time](/wiki/Time_After_Time_(American_TV_series) "Time After Time (American TV series)")*, based on [the 1979 film](/wiki/Time_After_Time_(1979_film) "Time After Time (1979 film)"), H. G. Wells is portrayed by [Freddie Stroma](/wiki/Freddie_Stroma "Freddie Stroma").[[174]](#cite_note-174)
* In the 2019 television adaptation of *[The War of the Worlds](/wiki/The_War_of_the_Worlds_(British_TV_series) "The War of the Worlds (British TV series)")*, the character of "George", played by [Rafe Spall](/wiki/Rafe_Spall "Rafe Spall"), demonstrates a number of elements of Wells's own life, including his estrangement from his wife and unmarried co-habitation with the character of "Amy".[[175]](#cite_note-175)
* Peter Mikhail portrays Wells twice in the [Canadian television](/wiki/CBC_Television "CBC Television") period [detective series](/wiki/Detective_fiction "Detective fiction") [Murdoch Mysteries](/wiki/Murdoch_Mysteries "Murdoch Mysteries"), in episode 8 of season 3 "[Future Imperfect](/wiki/List_of_Murdoch_Mysteries_episodes#Season_3_(2010) "List of Murdoch Mysteries episodes")" (6 April 2010) and in episode 11 of season 13 "[Staring Blindly into the Future](/wiki/List_of_Murdoch_Mysteries_episodes#Season_13_(2019–2020) "List of Murdoch Mysteries episodes")" (13 January 2020).
* Wells is played by [Nick Cave](/wiki/Nick_Cave "Nick Cave") in the 2021 film *[The Electrical Life of Louis Wain](/wiki/The_Electrical_Life_of_Louis_Wain "The Electrical Life of Louis Wain")*.[[176]](#cite_note-176)

## Film adaptations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=H._G._Wells&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Film adaptations")]

The novels and short stories of H. G. Wells have been adapted for cinema. These include *[Island of Lost Souls](/wiki/Island_of_Lost_Souls_(1932_film) "Island of Lost Souls (1932 film)")* (1932), *[The Invisible Man](/wiki/The_Invisible_Man_(1933_film) "The Invisible Man (1933 film)")* (1933), *[Things to Come](/wiki/Things_to_Come "Things to Come")* (1936), *[The Man Who Could Work Miracles](/wiki/The_Man_Who_Could_Work_Miracles "The Man Who Could Work Miracles")* (1937), *[The War of the Worlds](/wiki/The_War_of_the_Worlds_(1953_film) "The War of the Worlds (1953 film)")* (1953), *[The Time Machine](/wiki/The_Time_Machine_(1960_film) "The Time Machine (1960 film)")* (1960), *[First Men in the Moon](/wiki/First_Men_in_the_Moon_(1964_film) "First Men in the Moon (1964 film)")* (1964), *[The Island of Dr. Moreau](/wiki/The_Island_of_Dr._Moreau_(1977_film) "The Island of Dr. Moreau (1977 film)")* (1977),  *[Time After Time](/wiki/Time_After_Time_(1979_film) "Time After Time (1979 film)")*  (1979), *[The Island of Dr. Moreau](/wiki/The_Island_of_Dr._Moreau_(1996_film) "The Island of Dr. Moreau (1996 film)")* (1996), *[The Time Machine](/wiki/The_Time_Machine_(2002_film) "The Time Machine (2002 film)")* (2002), *[War of the Worlds](/wiki/War_of_the_Worlds_(2005_film) "War of the Worlds (2005 film)")* (2005) and *[War of the Worlds](/wiki/War_of_the_Worlds_(2025_film) "War of the Worlds (2025 film)")* (2025).[[177]](#cite_note-177)[[178]](#cite_note-178)[[179]](#cite_note-179)[[180]](#cite_note-180)

## Literary papers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=H._G._Wells&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Literary papers")]

In 1954, the [University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_Urbana-Champaign "University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign") purchased the H. G. Wells literary papers and correspondence collection.[[181]](#cite_note-library.illinois.edu-181) The university's [Rare Book & Manuscript Library](/wiki/The_Rare_Book_%26_Manuscript_Library_(University_of_Illinois_at_Urbana-Champaign) "The Rare Book & Manuscript Library (University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign)") holds the largest collection of Wells manuscripts, correspondence, first editions and publications in the United States.[[182]](#cite_note-182) Among these is unpublished material and the manuscripts of such works as *The War of the Worlds* and *The Time Machine*. The collection includes first editions, revisions and translations. The letters contain general family correspondence, communications from publishers, material regarding the Fabian Society, and letters from politicians and public figures, most notably [George Bernard Shaw](/wiki/George_Bernard_Shaw "George Bernard Shaw") and [Joseph Conrad](/wiki/Joseph_Conrad "Joseph Conrad").[[181]](#cite_note-library.illinois.edu-181)
